title: What Changes Across ResNet Variants
concept: Across ResNet variants, the main knobs that change are depth, block type, width, and compute budget, while the core residual idea — learn a transformation and add it to a shortcut path — stays the same.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the shared core idea across basic-block and bottleneck ResNets?

options.text: A residual shortcut carries input forward while the main path learns an added transformation
Every variant must have the same depth and width

exercises.explain: Right — that residual add-back pattern is the family constant. The variants mostly change the surrounding budget choices.

cards.title: One chassis, several knobs
cards.body: Once you stop thinking of ResNets as a list of famous names, they become easier to reason about. A variant is usually the same general residual recipe with a few knobs turned: more blocks, different block interiors, wider channels, or a different compute target.

cards.title: What actually varies
cards.body: The biggest changes across ResNet variants are usually depth (how many blocks), block type (basic or bottleneck), width (how many channels per stage), and therefore compute budget. What usually does not change is the core residual structure: a shortcut plus a learned path.

cards.title: Same car platform, different trims
cards.body: A car maker may sell compact, sport, and long-range versions of the same platform. The wheelbase, motor size, and battery can change, but the chassis logic stays recognizable. ResNet variants are similar: same residual platform, different resource tradeoffs.

cards.title: Choosing for a budget
cards.body: If you want a lighter backbone, you might choose a shallower or narrower ResNet. If you can afford more compute and want richer capacity, you might step up to a deeper bottleneck variant. The decision is less about 'which model name sounds coolest' and more about how much depth and width your budget can support.

cards.title: A new variant is not automatically a new species
cards.body: People sometimes talk as if every named ResNet variant represents a totally different architectural philosophy. Often that is overstated. Many variants are still recognizably ResNet because the residual shortcut pattern survives; what changed is how aggressively the family spends compute.

cards.title: What changes vs. what stays
cards.body: A helpful mental split is fixed core vs. adjustable knobs.

left.text:
Residual shortcut idea
Main path learns a transformation
Block outputs are added to the shortcut

right.text:
Depth, block type, width
Stage sizes and compute budget
How heavy the backbone becomes

exercises.prompt: Which set of factors best describes what commonly changes across ResNet variants?

options.text: Depth, block style, width, and compute budget
Only the icon emoji and file extension
The existence of residual shortcuts themselves
Whether the model is still a CNN

exercises.explain: Exactly — those are the real architecture knobs.

exercises.prompt: True or false: even when width or depth changes, a model can still clearly belong to the ResNet family if the residual shortcut pattern remains central.
exercises.explain: True — family membership is mostly about the residual design logic.

exercises.sentence: Across the family, the residual ___ is the part that usually stays conceptually constant.
exercises.explain: Right — the shortcut-plus-main-path structure is the anchor idea.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best captures the right big-picture view of ResNet variants?

options.text: They are mostly different ways of spending depth and compute around one shared residual backbone idea
They are unrelated architectures that only happen to share a word in the title
They differ only in training data and never in architecture

exercises.explain: Yes — that view is much more useful than memorizing model names in isolation.

exercises.prompt: Suppose two teams both want a ResNet backbone. Team A needs something lighter and faster; Team B can afford more compute for a deeper feature extractor. What is the most accurate architectural way to describe their choices?

options.text: They can stay in the same ResNet family while choosing different depth, block type, and width settings under different compute budgets
They must leave the ResNet family entirely because a family cannot support two budgets
They should ignore residual structure and choose only by whichever model has the highest number
They need completely different learning paradigms because residual blocks cannot scale

exercises.explain: Exactly. Variants are really budget-aware versions of the same residual design philosophy. That is a big reason the family became so useful in practice. Next, we'll ask why ResNet turned into such a common default backbone across computer vision.
